summary: Markets closed higher on Wall Street, driven primarily by tech sector gains and cautious optimism surrounding ongoing U.S.-Iran peace negotiations, even as European shares fell to a one-week low amid deepening Middle East uncertainty. The dominant macro forces shaping sentiment include fragile ceasefire dynamics between Israel and Hezbollah, active U.S.-Iran diplomatic talks through intermediaries, and record U.S. crude exports in May as the Iran conflict continues to tighten global oil supplies. On the stock and sector front, technology led gains, FedEx Freight received a fresh buy rating following its spinoff, and General Motors faces mounting pressure as a UAW strike threatens truck production, while the dollar strengthened as traders parsed Middle East developments. Key risk factors commanding investor attention include the potential collapse of Iran negotiations, Hormuz Strait shipping disruptions, a threatened Norwegian oil worker strike affecting roughly 8% of the workforce, the ongoing Russian threat of a massive strike on Ukraine, and growing supply constraints as U.S. manufacturing activity hits a four-year high. The short-term outlook remains cautiously constructive, with prediction markets signaling a stronger-than-expected May jobs report, but geopolitical flashpoints across the Middle East and Eastern Europe could rapidly shift sentiment and inject volatility into energy markets and risk assets.
